title: Howl's Moving Castle - Chapter Nine: In which Michael has trouble with a spell (Fragment 1)
text:
Howl‚Äôs Moving Castle

Chapter Nine

I N WHICH M ICHAEL HAS TROUBLE WITH A SPELL

It was the sea captain at the door, come for his wind spell at last, and not at all pleased at having to wait. ‚ÄúIf I miss my tide, boy,‚Äù he said to Michael, ‚ÄúI shall have a word with the Sorcerer about you. I don‚Äôt like lazy boys.‚Äù

Michael, in Sophie‚Äôs opinion, was far too polite to him, but she was feeling too dejected to interfere. When the captain had gone, Michael went to the bench to frown over his spell again and Sophie sat silently mending her stockings. She had only the one pair and her knobby feet had worn huge holes in them. Her gray dress by this time was frayed and dirty. She wondered whether she dared cut the least-stained bits out of Howl‚Äôs ruined blue-and-silver suit to make herself a new skirt with. But she did not quite dare.

‚ÄúSophie,‚Äù Michael said, looking up from his eleventh page of notes, ‚Äúhow many nieces have you?‚Äù

Sophie had been afraid Michael would start asking questions. ‚ÄúWhen you get to my age, my lad,‚Äù she said, ‚Äúyou lose count. They all look so alike. Those two Letties could be twins, to my mind.‚Äù

‚ÄúOh, no, not really,‚Äù Michael said, to her surprise. ‚ÄúThe niece in Upper Folding isn‚Äôt as pretty as my Lettie.‚Äù He tore up the eleventh page and made a twelfth. ‚ÄúI‚Äôm glad Howl didn‚Äôt meet my Lettie,‚Äù he said. He began on his thirteenth page and tore that up too. ‚ÄúI wanted to laugh when that Mrs. Fairfax said she knew who Howl was, didn‚Äôt you?‚Äù

‚ÄúNo,‚Äù said Sophie. It had made no difference to Lettie‚Äôs feelings. She thought of Lettie‚Äôs bright, adoring face under the apple blossom. ‚ÄúI suppose there‚Äôs no chance,‚Äù she asked hopelessly, ‚Äúthat Howl could be properly in love this time?‚Äù

Calcifer snorted green sparks up the chimney.

‚ÄúI was afraid you‚Äôd start thinking that,‚Äù Michael said. ‚ÄúBut you‚Äôd be deceiving yourself, just like Mrs. Fairfax.‚Äù

‚ÄúHow do you know?‚Äù said Sophie.

Calcifer and Michael exchanged glances. ‚ÄúDid he forget to spend at least an hour in the bathroom this morning?‚Äù Michael asked.

‚ÄúHe was in there two hours,‚Äù said Calcifer, ‚Äúputting spells on his face. Vain fool!‚Äù

‚ÄúThere you are, then,‚Äù said Michael. ‚ÄúThe day Howl forgets to do that will be the day I believe he‚Äôs really in love, and not before.‚Äù

Sophie thought of Howl on one knee in the orchard, posing to look as handsome as possible, and she knew they were right. She thought of going to the bathroom and tipping all Howl‚Äôs beauty spells down the toilet. But she did not quite dare. Instead, she hobbled up and fetched the blue-and-silver suit, which she spent the rest of the day cutting little blue triangles out of in order to make a patchwork sort of skirt.

Michael patted her shoulder kindly as he came to throw all seventeen pages of his notes onto Calcifer. ‚ÄúEveryone gets over things in the end, you know,‚Äù he said.

By this time it was clear Michael was having trouble with his spell. He gave up the notes and scraped some soot off the chimney. Calcifer craned round to watch him in a mystified way. Michael took a withered root from one of the bags hanging on the beams and put it in the soot. Then, after much thought, he turned the doorknob blue-down and vanished for twenty minutes into Porthaven. He came back with a large, whorled seashell and put that with the root and the soot. After that he tore up pages and pages of paper and put those in too. He put the lot in front of the human skull and stood blowing on it, so that soot and bits of paper whirled all over the bench.

meta.author: Diana Wynne Jones
meta.sectionTitle: Chapter Nine: In which Michael has trouble with a spell

vocabulary.definition: Showing great love or admiration.
vocabulary.example: Lettie‚Äôs bright, adoring face under the apple blossom.

vocabulary.definition: Describing the combined colours blue and silver.
vocabulary.example: Howl‚Äôs ruined blue-and-silver suit.

vocabulary.definition: A routine task or job.
Stretch out one's neck in order to see better.
vocabulary.example: Calcifer craned round to watch him.

vocabulary.definition: Sad and depressed; dispirited.
vocabulary.example: She was feeling too dejected to interfere.

vocabulary.definition: Worn away or ragged at the edges through constant rubbing or use.
vocabulary.example: Her gray dress by this time was frayed and dirty.

vocabulary.definition: To wear away the edge or surface of something by rubbing.
vocabulary.example: Her dress was frayed after much use.

vocabulary.definition: To move along awkwardly, especially with a limp.
vocabulary.example: She hobbled up and fetched the suit.

vocabulary.definition: Having knobs or rounded lumps; knobbly.
vocabulary.example: Her knobby feet had worn huge holes in them.

vocabulary.definition: To make sounds and movements of the face that express amusement.
To repair or fix something that is broken or torn.
The act of repairing stockings by sewing.
Utterly bewildered or perplexed.
vocabulary.example: Calcifer watched him in a mystified way.

vocabulary.definition: Written records or brief jottings about something.
vocabulary.example: Seventeen pages of notes on his spell.

vocabulary.definition: Needlework in which small pieces of cloth in different designs and colours are sewn together.
vocabulary.example: She made a patchwork skirt from little blue triangles.

vocabulary.definition: Applying magical magic charms or incantations.
vocabulary.example: He was putting spells on his face.

vocabulary.definition: To rub something hard or sharp against a surface, removing dirt or material.
vocabulary.example: He scraped some soot off the chimney.

vocabulary.definition: Detecting or perceiving a scent.
A black powdery or flaky substance consisting largely of amorphous carbon, produced by the incomplete burning of organic matter.
vocabulary.example: He scraped some soot off the chimney.

vocabulary.definition: A form of words used as a magical charm or incantation.
vocabulary.example: Michael was having trouble with his spell again.

vocabulary.definition: Ripped into pieces.
Two children born at the same birth.
vocabulary.example: Those two Letties could be twins.

vocabulary.definition: To disappear suddenly and completely.
vocabulary.example: He vanished for twenty minutes.

vocabulary.definition: To disappear suddenly and completely.
vocabulary.example: He vanished for twenty minutes into Porthaven.

vocabulary.definition: Indicates inclusion or accompaniment.
vocabulary.example: He put the root and soot together with the seashell.

vocabulary.definition: Dried up or shriveled, typically due to loss of moisture.
vocabulary.example: Withered root from one of the bags hanging on the beams.
